title: Intermittent 401s for service principals after ingress SNI host-rewrite causes tenant claim misrouting in private deployment
preview:
Issue summary: Several service principals (machine-to-machine tokens) started receiving intermittent 401 responses when calling the Redwood Private ingestion endpoint behind the customer's edge proxy. The failures began after the customer's ops team rolled out a host-rewrite rule on their SNI-terminating proxy.

Impact: Authentication fails for automated jobs that call Redwood's admin/service APIs. Approximately 6% of requests from BlueWave's ingest workers fail with 401 over the past 8 hours, causing batch jobs to abort and delaying data pipelines. No user-facing impact reported, but multiple downstream workflows are impacted.

Environment: Private deployment (customer-managed control plane) in us-west region. Customer uses an external edge proxy that performs SNI termination and rewrites the Host header to route to internal tenants. Service principals authenticate using mutual TLS-backed JWTs (service tokens) with the tenant bound in the 'tenant' claim and in a forwarded header (X-Redwood-Tenant).
text:

The failures began after the customer's ops team rolled out a host-rewrite rule on their SNI-terminating proxy.

No user-facing impact reported, but multiple downstream workflows are impacted.

Environment: Private deployment (customer-managed control plane) in us-west region. Customer uses an external edge proxy that performs SNI termination and rewrites the Host header to route to internal tenants. Service principals authenticate using mutual TLS-backed JWTs (service tokens) with the tenant bound in the 'tenant' claim and in a forwarded header (X-Redwood-Tenant).

title: Private deployment: SSO token aud check fails when customer ingress preserves Host header via identity-proxy, causing 403 to admin APIs
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports sudden 403 authorization failures for admin/operator endpoints in their private VPC deployment after a weekend ingress upgrade. Symptoms align with SSO-authenticated sessions failing authorization checks despite tokens appearing valid at the IdP side. Impact: Admin console and API calls that require elevated RBAC roles are blocked for multiple operators; day-to-day ops impaired. 

Observed behavior: Calls authenticated via Azure AD SSO succeed at token issuance but are rejected by Redwood private control-plane with an explicit audience mismatch message during token validation. Error started after customer applied an ingress change which enabled "preserveHost: true" on their identity-proxy service.

Why this matters: For private deployments we validate token audience (aud) strictly against expected runtime client IDs. Changes in the perceived host/origin can cause the auth-proxy to present a different effective audience during introspection/validation, leading to authorization denial even though the IdP issued a valid token to the user application.
text:

Symptoms align with SSO-authenticated sessions failing authorization checks despite tokens appearing valid at the IdP side. Observed behavior: Calls authenticated via Azure AD SSO succeed at token issuance but are rejected by Redwood private control-plane with an explicit audience mismatch message during token validation. Error started after customer applied an ingress change which enabled "preserveHost: true" on their identity-proxy service.

Why this matters: For private deployments we validate token audience (aud) strictly against expected runtime client IDs. Changes in the perceived host/origin can cause the auth-proxy to present a different effective audience during introspection/validation, leading to authorization denial even though the IdP issued a valid token to the user application.

title: Sustained 429 spikes after credit reconciliation during simultaneous bulk backfill and low-latency webhooks
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer ScribeAI reports sustained 429 responses across a subset of API keys immediately following a tenant credit reconciliation event (automatic refill) that occurred 2026-03-10 00:05 UTC. The tenant was simultaneously running a long-running bulk embedding backfill and high-frequency webhook-driven chat inference for low-latency UI actions.

Impact:
- Customer sees 20-40% of requests returning 429 for ~3 hours after refill window.
- Real-time chat flows experienced user-visible failures; bulk backfill jobs retried and slowed throughput.
- Appears to affect only requests routed via the dedicated pool in us-east.

Requested outcome:
- Root cause analysis and mitigation to avoid recurrence during future credit refills and autosyncs.
- Guidance / recommended config to isolate bulk backfills from low-latency routes (rate classes, burst pools, or route separation).

title: Clarify accounting for sustained vs short-burst tokens with HTTP/2 connection reuse and signed-url multipath
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports repeated 429s during a sustained ingest job that uses multiplexed HTTP/2 clients and short-lived signed URLs. They see higher-than-expected throttle counts when reusing the same underlying connection across several ephemeral API keys and signed callbacks.

Impact:
- Intermittent 429s on 3 of 5 ingest workers, causing job retries and delayed backfill.
- Customer on a Dedicated pool; affects SLA and daily throughput.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool (prod-us-west) pinned to redwood/open-llama-13b; autoscaling disabled.
- Ingress: client uses HTTP/2 connection reuse with multiple multiplexed streams, each stream attaching a freshly requested signed URL token per upload chunk.

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Open a single persistent HTTP/2 connection from an orchestrator to our edge.
2) Spawn 20 concurrent streams over that connection.
3) For each stream, request a signed URL (short-lived key) and immediately POST chunked content to the signed path.
4) Repeat every ~30s for ~15 minutes.

Logs / Observations:
- Customer-provided logs show 429 responses clustered after ~7 minutes. 429 response headers include X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and X-RateLimit-Window: short-burst.
- Edge proxy logs show the same client IP mapping to a single connection with many active streams; signed-URL tokens are unique per request.
- Console quota view shows tenant tokens draining faster than expected; console displays per-route burst pool being consumed.

Customer hypothesis:
They believe token accounting is happening per-connection rather than per API key/signed-token and that connection reuse is causing multipath counting into a single short-burst pool. They ask whether signed URL callbacks or ephemeral tokens should be isolated from per-connection smoothing windows.

Investigation notes (support):
- Reproduced a simplified load pattern in staging: multiplexed HTTP/2 streams with ephemeral auth. Observed elevated short-burst consumption on the route layer.
- Initial tracing shows request attribution occurs at the ingress handler which binds rate accounting keys by (tenant_id, route, connection_fingerprint) when stream reuse and signedurl tokens are present. This aggregation was an optimization to reduce lookup overhead for long-lived sessions.
- Confirmed that in current config a single connection_fingerprint can cause burst credits to be shared across ephemeral tokens presented on separate streams.

Hypothesis and risk assessment:
- The aggregation behavior is intentional in high-throughput paths to avoid per-request auth lookups, but it can lead to surprising tenant-burst exhaustion when many short-lived tokens are fanned over a single connection.
- Risk: dedicated customers running multiplexed orchestrators (like serverless uploader pools) may hit short-burst exhaustion even if logical API keys are within limits.

Workaround provided to customer:
- Recommend switching the orchestrator to open multiple shorter-lived connections (reduce stream fanout per connection) to ensure connection_fingerprint separation.
- Alternative: request signed URLs with slightly longer TTL and reduce frequency of refreshes so requests fall into sustained refill accounting rather than short-burst.
- Temporary console change: increased per-route short-burst cap for their dedicated pool while we investigate (change applied at 2026-03-10T18:40:00Z).

Proposed permanent fixes / next steps:
1) Product: add configuration flag on Dedicated pools to prefer per-token accounting over connection-aggregation for routes flagged as "ephemeral-signedurl".
2) Engineering: evaluate trade-offs and implement an opt-in mode that performs a fast token bind (cache per-token) when a signed-url header is detected, avoiding connection-based aggregation.
3) SRE: run a canary in staging that flips the flag for one edge shard and run load tests to verify performance and quota accounting correctness.

Resolution timeline request by customer:
- Customer requests a low-risk fix or a documented recommended architecture within 2 weeks to avoid re-architecting their ingestion pipeline.

Additional notes:
- Related internal discussion in #support and #infra-edge about connection-level optimizations.
- Billing/usage shows consistent token burn matching the described pattern.

title: Parallel model shard downloads fail with 403s due to CGNAT ephemeral port collisions on customer's egress path
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent 403s and corrupted ranged downloads when their private deployment attempts to fetch model shards in parallel (10-20 concurrent range GETs). Failures only occur when the customer's egress path transits a carrier-grade NAT (CGNAT) device provided by their ISP.

Impact:
- Production model loads failing ~15-40% of parallel shard fetch attempts, causing increased model startup latency and occasional inference timeouts for user requests.
- Errors are presigned-url 403s and occasional HTTP range truncation requiring retry.

Environment:
- Customer: DataQube Analytics (dedicated private deploy, VPC peered to redwood control plane)
- Region: us-east
- Serving nodes: rwinf-dedicated-ssd-gpu-32 (pool of 6)
- Model: gpt-large-v2 (sharded objects, parallel range GETs)

title: SSE stream drops intermediate sentence during mobile network handoff (Wi‑Fi→Cellular)
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that when a mobile client switches networks mid-stream (Wi‑Fi → Cellular) the SSE session reconnect completes, but the stream restarts from a later token and an entire intermediate sentence is dropped. The resumed stream includes tokens that make the final output syntactically correct but missing a whole clause from the middle of the response.

Impact:
- Affects a small subset of enterprise customers using long streaming responses on mobile apps.
- User-visible missing sentences in generated content (integrated note-taking app) leading to content correctness issues.
- Occurs intermittently during network handoffs; not blocking but causes unacceptable data loss for the customer (enterprise tier SLA).

Environment:
- Prod, us-east region behind our CDN + cloud LB (customer uses iOS app 16.x and Android 13).
- Client uses EventSource (SSE) via our JS shim (redwood-client-js v1.4.2) with automatic reconnect.
- Typical responses: 800–1200 tokens.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Start a streaming SSE generation from the app with a long prompt (expected >600 tokens).
2. While stream is active on Wi‑Fi, toggle device to airplane mode briefly then re-enable (or force switch to cellular).
3. Observe that client reconnects and receives a resumed stream but the middle sentence of the earlier content is missing.

Observed logs (selected):
- Gateway: [2026-03-05T13:22:17Z] conn=abcd1234 handoff detected, upstream=cdn-edge-7 GOAWAY
- Edge: [2026-03-05T13:22:17Z] session_token=st-98765 swapped -> st-98765-2, mapping_lookup OK
- Serving: [2026-03-05T13:22:17Z] resume_offset=11234, kv_cache_validation=FAIL (expected_prefix_hash!=observed)
- Client SDK: reconnect eventId=evt-3105, last_received_offset=11102

Notes: the delta between last_received_offset (client) and resume_offset (server) maps to ~1 sentence (≈150 tokens) and kv_cache_validation returned FAIL which triggers the runtime to fast-forward to a safe continuation point; that fast-forward point is after the missing sentence.

Attachments:
- HAR and EventSource logs supplied by customer (attached in ticket)
- CDN and LB traces were linked in comments below.

Desired outcome:
- On network handoff/reconnect, resume behavior must preserve all previously delivered tokens or deliver a clear overlap/deduplication so no content is silently lost.
text:

title: SDK sidecar keepalive trickle results in suppressed final chunk for long-running SSE sessions
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that long-running SSE streams from our hosted endpoint sometimes arrive to their client with all content except the final concluding chunk. Impact: Final sentence or closing JSON metadata is missing in 1-3% of sessions for a specific mobile app version, causing truncated completions and downstream UI to show a perpetual 'typing' indicator. Environment: Dedicated customer (Acme Retail) using Redwood hosted API via the mobile SDK v1.8.3 that runs a local sidecar process to multiplex network activity on flaky mobile connections.

Observed behavior differs from previous SSE truncation tickets because the server completes generation and emits the end event (visible in backend logs), but the client never receives the terminal chunk in a subset of reconnect/resume scenarios.
text:

Impact: Final sentence or closing JSON metadata is missing in 1-3% of sessions for a specific mobile app version, causing truncated completions and downstream UI to show a perpetual 'typing' indicator. Environment: Dedicated customer (Acme Retail) using Redwood hosted API via the mobile SDK v1.8.3 that runs a local sidecar process to multiplex network activity on flaky mobile connections.

Observed behavior differs from previous SSE truncation tickets because the server completes generation and emits the end event (visible in backend logs), but the client never receives the terminal chunk in a subset of reconnect/resume scenarios.

title: ScribeHQ: streaming responses sometimes end early (partial output, no error)
preview:
Customer reports streaming outputs occasionally terminate early with no explicit error. They receive an apparently clean end-of-stream but the assistant text is truncated relative to expected completion length.

Initial signal came via email thread in Ben Carter mailbox (20260303-scribehq-stream-ends-early.json).

Customer details:
- Region: us-east
- Client uses HTTP/2
- Provided HAR capture (har-capture-sse.json) and request_id req_c41a...

Hypotheses:
- upstream connection reset or GOAWAY causing client to treat stream as complete
- gateway incorrectly emitting [DONE] on backend cancellation

Asks to customer:
- raw response headers
- confirm whether they ever see incomplete JSON/event framing
- confirm whether they are using any proxy (e.g., Cloudflare)

title: Microburst smoothing credit omission after tag-normalization change caused missing invoice credit
preview: Issue summary: Customer reports that a $3,120 smoothing credit (hourly buffer credit) that usually offsets microburst reprocessing was not applied on the finalized invoice for 2026-02-28. Impact: VocalIQ sees an unexpected invoice line of $3,120; customer is on Dedicated capacity and expects smoothing credits for transient microburst replays. Environment: prod-us-east, dedicated cluster vocaliq-ded-1. They executed a scheduled reprocess batch (microbatch job) between 02:10-02:26 UTC that retried ~18k requests. Expected: hourly smoothing buffer credit appears as a negative line item and offsets reprocess charges. Observed: no smoothing credit; charges appear at full rate and no provisional credit was applied. The customer is requesting an explanation and provisional credit while we investigate.

Expected: hourly smoothing buffer credit appears as a negative line item and offsets reprocess charges. Observed: no smoothing credit; charges appear at full rate and no provisional credit was applied. The customer is requesting an explanation and provisional credit while we investigate.

title: SDK exponential backoff jitter misconfiguration causing serialized retries and apparent inference timeouts for bulk requests
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a spike in end-to-end inference latency and client-side timeouts when sending large batched inference requests (bulk job of ~25 concurrent requests per client). The SDK appears to serialize retries and produces long head-of-line delays rather than distributing retry attempts across healthy connections.

Impact:
- Several customers on Dedicated capacity experienced perceived timeouts and increased median latency for batched/parallel inference.
- Client-side metrics show heavy retry activity and a single active TCP connection per SDK process during peak bursts.
- Affects production workloads for PromptCraft (dedicated allocation).

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated (prod us-east-1)
- Customer SDK: redwood-sdk-js v2.3.0 (custom config)
- Load pattern: ~25 parallel connections from a single process batching 16 prompts per request

Observed behavior:
- Burst of requests -> SDK initiates retries but retry jitter misconfiguration causes uniform backoff windows (no randomness), leading to synchronized retry waves.
- SDK default maxConnections overridden to 1 in customer's init script (corporate template), leading to serialized HTTP requests.
- API gateway reports many concurrent queued requests from the same tenant but only one open upstream connection, producing head-of-line blocking and increased latency.
- Clients observe socket-level timeouts and then exponential retries in lockstep, which appear as repeated long latencies rather than occasional failed calls.

Expected behavior:
- SDK should spread retries across connections with randomized jitter so retries are staggered, and clients should use a higher connection pool for parallel workloads.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Start a single-node test client using redwood-sdk-js v2.3.0 with maxConnections set to 1 and backoff config: initial=200ms, multiplier=2, jitter=0 (explicitly disabled).
2) Fire 25 parallel request tasks, each sending a batch payload of 12 prompts to redwood-7b-v1 on prod us-east-1.
3) Induce a transient upstream 503 on serving-runtime (simulate slow node or canary drain) for ~3s.
4) Observe client-side request queueing, repeated retries all happening in same time-window, and increased p95/p99 latencies.

Logs / traces collected:
- API gateway trace id: agw-us-east-20260301-7f9a2c (attached in ticket)
- Client logs show repeated "Retrying request (attempt 3) after backoff=1200ms" with identical timestamps across multiple tasks.
- Serving-runtime logs show spikes of queued requests from tenant PromptCraft and repeated 502/503s during the window.

Notes:
- Customer applied an internal SDK wrapper that set maxConnections=1 for connection pinning to their telemetry proxy. That policy combined with disabled jitter caused serialized retry storms.
- Not a server-only regression: reproduces locally with the customer's SDK config.

title: Resume subprotocol mismatch causes session rebind and drops terminal attachment on reconnect
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that after a brief network blip their websocket stream reconnects but the final embedded attachment (base64 PNG) is missing and the assistant sends duplicated system messages. The application sees a successful 101 upgrade on reconnect yet the streamed session does not resume semantics and the last 'attachment' chunk is never emitted.

Impact:
- ParcelFlow's invoice-generation flow fails to attach generated receipt images to the final response for ~2% of streamed requests during the reconnect window.
- End users see duplicated system messages ("System: Session resumed") and a truncated output (missing base64 attachment).

Environment:
- Customer on Dedicated cluster (prod, us-east) behind corporate edge proxy (vendor: Korunner WAF) and an internal ALB.
- SDK: redwood-js v1.4.2 using websocket with sec-websocket-protocol: "redwood-stream-v1" set by client.
- Model: gpt-open-3b pinned for route.

Steps to reproduce (reported by customer):
1) Start a websocket streaming request that yields an image embed near the end (attachment as base64 in final chunk).
2) Induce a short TCP path migration (client wifi -> cellular).
3) Client reconnects and re-sends the original session token (resume_token) but the websocket handshake goes through a proxy that rewrites/normalizes the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header to "redwood-stream".
4) Stream resumes successfully at the network layer but no final embed chunk is received; instead two "System: resumed" messages appear and the stream terminates.

Observed behavior:
- Server logs show a new session created for the reconnect instead of a resume: log line: "session_resume: failed -> subprotocol_mismatch, new_session_created=1".
- Edge proxy (ALB -> Korunner) dropped or normalized the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol during the upgrade, causing token-to-subprotocol mismatch in resume routing.
- The final attachment generation completed on the worker (worker logs show glue-attach emitted) but the transport-layer mapping to the original session id was lost; client never receives the terminal base64 chunk.

Expected behavior:
- Resume should be keyed primarily by session resume token and allow subprotocol variant normalization without creating a new session.
- If a true new session is required, server should stream a clear terminal event and not duplicate system messages.

Attachments/Logs:
- Customer supplied capture: websocket upgrade requests (pre/post) and a HAR showing sec-websocket-protocol header values.
- Edge logs: "ALB: removed header Sec-WebSocket-Protocol for sticky path 10.20.30.40".
- Worker log snippet: "[worker-7] emit: attachment(base64) id=attach-0312 status=ready -> queued_transport(session_map_lookup=miss)".
text:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that after a brief network blip their websocket stream reconnects but the final embedded attachment (base64 PNG) is missing and the assistant sends duplicated system messages. The application sees a successful 101 upgrade on reconnect yet the streamed session does not resume semantics and the last 'attachment' chunk is never emitted.

Impact:
- ParcelFlow's invoice-generation flow fails to attach generated receipt images to the final response for ~2% of streamed requests during the reconnect window.
- End users see duplicated system messages ("System: Session resumed") and a truncated output (missing base64 attachment).

Environment:
- Customer on Dedicated cluster (prod, us-east) behind corporate edge proxy (vendor: Korunner WAF) and an internal ALB.
- SDK: redwood-js v1.4.2 using websocket with sec-websocket-protocol: "redwood-stream-v1" set by client.
- Model: gpt-open-3b pinned for route.

Steps to reproduce (reported by customer):
1) Start a websocket streaming request that yields an image embed near the end (attachment as base64 in final chunk).
2) Induce a short TCP path migration (client wifi -> cellular).
3) Client reconnects and re-sends the original session token (resume_token) but the websocket handshake goes through a proxy that rewrites/normalizes the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header to "redwood-stream".
4) Stream resumes successfully at the network layer but no final embed chunk is received; instead two "System: resumed" messages appear and the stream terminates.

Observed behavior:
- Server logs show a new session created for the reconnect instead of a resume: log line: "session_resume: failed -> subprotocol_mismatch, new_session_created=1".
- Edge proxy (ALB -> Korunner) dropped or normalized the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol during the upgrade, causing token-to-subprotocol mismatch in resume routing.
- The final attachment generation completed on the worker (worker logs show glue-attach emitted) but the transport-layer mapping to the original session id was lost; client never receives the terminal base64 chunk.

Expected behavior:
- Resume should be keyed primarily by session resume token and allow subprotocol variant normalization without creating a new session.
- If a true new session is required, server should stream a clear terminal event and not duplicate system messages.

Attachments/Logs:
- Customer supplied capture: websocket upgrade requests (pre/post) and a HAR showing sec-websocket-protocol header values.
- Edge logs: "ALB: removed header Sec-WebSocket-Protocol for sticky path 10.20.30.40".
- Worker log snippet: "[worker-7] emit: attachment(base64) id=attach-0312 status=ready -> queued_transport(session_map_lookup=miss)".

title: Reserved burst credits not allocated to subaccount after plan downgrade during hourly billing window
preview: Issue summary: Customer reports that reserved burst credits (hourly burst capacity) were not applied to their subaccount after they downgraded a seat-level Dedicated plan in the middle of an hourly billing window. Impact: BrightCart observed a sudden unexpected invoice adjustment and a missing credit line for the subaccount, resulting in an overage alert and a triggered automated payment hold. Timeline: 2026-03-01 22:05 UTC - Customer changed plan (downgrade) on parent org. 2026-03-01 22:12 UTC - Scheduler ran hourly credit allocation job for burst capacity. 2026-03-01 22:20 UTC - Customer subaccount shows no burst credits applied; parent account shows partial allocation. 2026-03-02 08:00 UTC - Customer opened support ticket. Key question: why weren't burst credits allocated to the subaccount when the allocation job ran within the same hour as the downgrade?
text: Issue summary: Customer reports that reserved burst credits (hourly burst capacity) were not applied to their subaccount after they downgraded a seat-level Dedicated plan in the middle of an hourly billing window. Impact: BrightCart observed a sudden unexpected invoice adjustment and a missing credit line for the subaccount, resulting in an overage alert and a triggered automated payment hold. Timeline: 2026-03-01 22:05 UTC - Customer changed plan (downgrade) on parent org. 2026-03-01 22:12 UTC - Scheduler ran hourly credit allocation job for burst capacity. 2026-03-01 22:20 UTC - Customer subaccount shows no burst credits applied; parent account shows partial allocation. 2026-03-02 08:00 UTC - Customer opened support ticket. Key question: why weren't burst credits allocated to the subaccount when the allocation job ran within the same hour as the downgrade?

title: Intermittent 403s after group membership updates due to entitlement propagation lag in Private VPC
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 403 Forbidden responses from Redwood Private endpoints for certain users immediately after SCIM-driven group membership changes. The failures resolve after ~30–90 seconds.

Impact: Affects ~5% of authentication attempts for affected users immediately after admin changes; blocks customer admins from testing role changes and causes automated workflows to fail briefly.

Environment: Private deployment in customer's VPC (prod-us-east). IdP: Okta (SAML), SCIM provisioning enabled from Okta -> Redwood SCIM endpoint. RBAC model uses group->role bindings.

Steps to reproduce:
1) In Okta, add a user to the 'data-scientists' group which maps to 'model-infer' role in Redwood.
2) Wait until SCIM push completes (Okta shows success).
3) Immediately have user call the hosted API via Private endpoint. Observe 403 for first few calls.
4) Repeat: after ~30–90s the same calls succeed without token refresh.

Logs / Observations:
- Support logs show 403s with authz failure cause 'entitlement-not-found' and no 'role' claim in our authz cache within first minute after SCIM event.
- SCIM logs show 200 OK from Okta and successful provisioning events.
- Audit trail in RBAC service shows group change processed, but internal 'entitlement-index' timestamps lag behind SCIM event by 20–70s.
- No errors in SCIM connector; network latency to customer's VPC averages 45ms.

Preliminary hypothesis: SCIM provisioning is processed by the SCIM worker and writes memberships to the RBAC store, but the entitlement indexer (asynchronous) has a processing delay under load which causes authz checks to consult stale index until the indexer catches up. This race leads to transient 403s for newly added entitlements.
text:
Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 403 Forbidden responses from Redwood Private endpoints for certain users immediately after SCIM-driven group membership changes. The failures resolve after ~30–90 seconds.

Impact: Affects ~5% of authentication attempts for affected users immediately after admin changes; blocks customer admins from testing role changes and causes automated workflows to fail briefly.

Environment: Private deployment in customer's VPC (prod-us-east). IdP: Okta (SAML), SCIM provisioning enabled from Okta -> Redwood SCIM endpoint. RBAC model uses group->role bindings.

Steps to reproduce:
1) In Okta, add a user to the 'data-scientists' group which maps to 'model-infer' role in Redwood.
2) Wait until SCIM push completes (Okta shows success).
3) Immediately have user call the hosted API via Private endpoint. Observe 403 for first few calls.
4) Repeat: after ~30–90s the same calls succeed without token refresh.

Logs / Observations:
- Support logs show 403s with authz failure cause 'entitlement-not-found' and no 'role' claim in our authz cache within first minute after SCIM event.
- SCIM logs show 200 OK from Okta and successful provisioning events.
- Audit trail in RBAC service shows group change processed, but internal 'entitlement-index' timestamps lag behind SCIM event by 20–70s.
- No errors in SCIM connector; network latency to customer's VPC averages 45ms.

Preliminary hypothesis: SCIM provisioning is processed by the SCIM worker and writes memberships to the RBAC store, but the entitlement indexer (asynchronous) has a processing delay under load which causes authz checks to consult stale index until the indexer catches up. This race leads to transient 403s for newly added entitlements.

title: Cross-proxy normalization of idempotency header causing duplicate tool/function executions
preview: Customer reports repeated duplicate side-effecting tool/function invocations when using function-calling with retries. The customer is on a dedicated plan behind a corporate reverse proxy that normalizes headers (lowercasing, truncation of long header values, and replacing spaces with hyphens). NimbusAI sees duplicate webhook/tool calls on retry even though they supply stable idempotency keys in the SDK. Hypothesis: the proxy is rewriting/persistently normalizing the Idempotency header in-flight, so Redwood's dedupe layer receives a different header value on the retry path and treats it as a new request.
text: Customer reports repeated duplicate side-effecting tool/function invocations when using function-calling with retries. The customer is on a dedicated plan behind a corporate reverse proxy that normalizes headers (lowercasing, truncation of long header values, and replacing spaces with hyphens). NimbusAI sees duplicate webhook/tool calls on retry even though they supply stable idempotency keys in the SDK. Hypothesis: the proxy is rewriting/persistently normalizing the Idempotency header in-flight, so Redwood's dedupe layer receives a different header value on the retry path and treats it as a new request.

title: Traffic surge causes canary routing to bypass model-version pin for certain sessions
preview:
Issue summary: During a large QPS spike for NovaChat, a subset of requests that had been explicitly pinned to rw-instruct-3b-v2 began receiving responses from the canary variant (rw-instruct-3b-v2-canary).

Impact: Customer reported inconsistent outputs (semantically different completions) for user sessions pinned to a stable model version. This impacted a small percentage (~3-5%) of requests over a 20 minute window, causing visible UX regressions for high-value enterprise customers.

Environment: Production (us-east). Customer uses Dedicated cluster with reserved capacity and a model-version pin configured at the route-level via the Console and API.

Observed behaviour: Pin set in route configuration appears to have been ignored for some requests; those requests were routed to canary. Canary traffic was permitted by rollout policy, but pinned sessions were expected to be excluded.

Expected behaviour: Requests from sessions pinned to rw-instruct-3b-v2 should consistently route to that exact model variant until pin is removed or updated. Policy-based canary rollouts must not override explicit pins.
text:
Issue summary: During a large QPS spike for NovaChat, a subset of requests that had been explicitly pinned to rw-instruct-3b-v2 began receiving responses from the canary variant (rw-instruct-3b-v2-canary).

Impact: Customer reported inconsistent outputs (semantically different completions) for user sessions pinned to a stable model version. This impacted a small percentage (~3-5%) of requests over a 20 minute window, causing visible UX regressions for high-value enterprise customers.

Environment: Production (us-east). Customer uses Dedicated cluster with reserved capacity and a model-version pin configured at the route-level via the Console and API.

Observed behaviour: Pin set in route configuration appears to have been ignored for some requests; those requests were routed to canary. Canary traffic was permitted by rollout policy, but pinned sessions were expected to be excluded.

Expected behaviour: Requests from sessions pinned to rw-instruct-3b-v2 should consistently route to that exact model variant until pin is removed or updated. Policy-based canary rollouts must not override explicit pins.

title: Streaming responses freeze mid-generation for Arcadia Health (SSE stalls, request never completes)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports streaming chat responses intermittently stop mid-generation. Client keeps the HTTP connection open; server stops emitting SSE tokens and never sends [DONE].

Impact:
- Production incident for Arcadia Health’s support-agent. End users see partial completions and stuck spinners.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool in us-east.
- Client uses OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Start streaming completion with max_tokens ~800.
2) After ~10–40 seconds, stream stalls mid-sentence.
3) No further bytes, no close, no error.

Investigation notes:
- Correlates with longer generations + high concurrent streams.
- Gateway shows upstream still open; runtime logs show request context cancelled late or not at all.
- Suspected backpressure interaction between token streamer + gzip + chunked transfer.

Workaround:
- Customer can set client-side read timeout and retry with idempotency key.

Resolution:
- Rolled out gateway change: disable gzip for SSE responses and flush after each token chunk.
- Added runtime guard: heartbeat/flush every 2s while streaming.

References:
- Slack: #support thread_ts 1769084201
- Customer emails in Ava Chen mailbox (thread_id gth_9a3f1b0a)
text:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports streaming chat responses intermittently stop mid-generation. Client keeps the HTTP connection open; server stops emitting SSE tokens and never sends [DONE].

Impact:
- Production incident for Arcadia Health’s support-agent. End users see partial completions and stuck spinners.

- Suspected backpressure interaction between token streamer + gzip + chunked transfer.

Resolution:

- Added runtime guard: heartbeat/flush every 2s while streaming.

References:

- Customer emails in Ava Chen mailbox (thread_id gth_9a3f1b0a)

title: Python SDK sends 'Bearer Bearer' in Authorization header for some configs
preview: Issue summary: Customer upgraded to redwood-sdk-python v0.9.4 and began receiving 401 Unauthorized. Their proxy logs show Authorization header is 'Bearer Bearer <key>' (double prefix). They previously passed api_key='Bearer <key>' (historical pattern from other vendors). Impact: auth failures in one service until rollback. Steps: (1) Initialize client with api_key that already includes 'Bearer ' (2) SDK adds Bearer prefix again (3) API rejects. Expected: SDK should accept raw key only OR detect and avoid double prefix and warn.
text: Issue summary: Customer upgraded to redwood-sdk-python v0.9.4 and began receiving 401 Unauthorized. Their proxy logs show Authorization header is 'Bearer Bearer <key>' (double prefix). They previously passed api_key='Bearer <key>' (historical pattern from other vendors). Impact: auth failures in one service until rollback. Steps: (1) Initialize client with api_key that already includes 'Bearer ' (2) SDK adds Bearer prefix again (3) API rejects. Expected: SDK should accept raw key only OR detect and avoid double prefix and warn.

title: Tenant routed to secondary variant despite explicit model pin during orchestrator retry surge
preview: Issue summary: One enterprise customer observed tenant traffic being routed to a secondary (fallback) variant even though their project had an explicit model version pin. Impact: customer-facing production traffic experienced degraded model behavior and increased cost due to falling back to a larger, higher-cost variant. This started during a rolling canary of a separate release when orchestrator retry activity spiked.
text: Issue summary: One enterprise customer observed tenant traffic being routed to a secondary (fallback) variant even though their project had an explicit model version pin. Impact: customer-facing production traffic experienced degraded model behavior and increased cost due to falling back to a larger, higher-cost variant. This started during a rolling canary of a separate release when orchestrator retry activity spiked.

title: token accounting discrepancy between streaming usage and invoice export
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a token accounting discrepancy: the Usage page (streaming chat route) shows ~18% more billable tokens than what they compute from request/response logs. Their invoice export CSV matches the higher number.

Impact:
Customer is pausing rollout until they can reconcile token counts for February.

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Call /v1/chat/completions with streaming enabled
2) Sum prompt_tokens + completion_tokens from the final chunk they receive
3) Compare to billable tokens shown in Console > Usage and invoice export

Notes:
- Customer claims some requests show completion_tokens=0 in their logs while they still received text
- They suspect retransmits / reconnects

Requested outcome:
Explain what counts as billable tokens for streaming and whether retries can double-count.

title: KV cache serialization spike causes backpressure and client timeouts on deep prompts with high max_tokens
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer LumaDocs reports repeated request timeouts (504 / client disconnects) when sending prompts with very large context + max_tokens=120000 via the Redwood hosted API. Occurs primarily for streams that use function-calling and chunked streaming.

Impact:
- Several production API calls for LumaDocs are timing out during long-document summarization jobs, causing failed jobs and user-facing errors.
- Error rate ~12% for affected routes over a 6-hour window; spikes align with heavy serialization activity on the serving nodes.

Environment:
- Hosted (Redwood managed), dedicated tenant for LumaDocs
- Region: us-east
- Models: redwood-alpha-1 (default), gptj-6b used for fallback testing

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Send a streaming generation request with a prompt consisting of concatenated OCRed pages (~90k tokens) plus system instructions (function-calls enabled).
2) Set max_tokens to 120000 and enable streaming with chunk_size=1024.
3) Observe response flow: initial tokens stream, then long pause (~20-60s), then 504 or client disconnect.

Observed behavior:
- Serving-runtime logs show a sharp CPU spike on the inference host coincident with a call to kv-cache serialize/flush.
- During the spike, the request is held in the finalizer queue; downstream router returns 504 or the client times out.
- When reproducing in a dedicated test cluster, increasing the CPU limit on the host delays but does not eliminate timeouts.

Expected behavior:
- Long-context requests should stream tokens progressively; KV cache maintenance should not block final token flushing or cause client-visible timeouts.

Logs and traces:
- Sample trace: https://observability.internal/redwood/traces/trace-2025-01-17-22abcd
- Example serving log excerpt: 
  [2025-01-17T16:42:12Z] INFO finalizer: start finalize request SUP-531907-ctx-123
  [2025-01-17T16:42:30Z] WARN kv-cache: beginning bulk-serialize (size=512MB, entries=19278)
  [2025-01-17T16:42:55Z] ERROR api-gateway: upstream timeout after 30s while waiting for response
  [2025-01-17T16:43:02Z] INFO finalizer: aborted due to client disconnect

Additional notes:
- No concurrent autoscaler events recorded for the node.
- Node has nvme-backed local disk; serialization writes are synchronous to disk in current runtime build.
text:
Issue summary:
Customer LumaDocs reports repeated request timeouts (504 / client disconnects) when sending prompts with very large context + max_tokens=120000 via the Redwood hosted API. Occurs primarily for streams that use function-calling and chunked streaming.

Impact:
- Several production API calls for LumaDocs are timing out during long-document summarization jobs, causing failed jobs and user-facing errors.
- Error rate ~12% for affected routes over a 6-hour window; spikes align with heavy serialization activity on the serving nodes.

Environment:
- Hosted (Redwood managed), dedicated tenant for LumaDocs
- Region: us-east
- Models: redwood-alpha-1 (default), gptj-6b used for fallback testing

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Send a streaming generation request with a prompt consisting of concatenated OCRed pages (~90k tokens) plus system instructions (function-calls enabled).
2) Set max_tokens to 120000 and enable streaming with chunk_size=1024.
3) Observe response flow: initial tokens stream, then long pause (~20-60s), then 504 or client disconnect.

Observed behavior:
- Serving-runtime logs show a sharp CPU spike on the inference host coincident with a call to kv-cache serialize/flush.
- During the spike, the request is held in the finalizer queue; downstream router returns 504 or the client times out.
- When reproducing in a dedicated test cluster, increasing the CPU limit on the host delays but does not eliminate timeouts.

Expected behavior:
- Long-context requests should stream tokens progressively; KV cache maintenance should not block final token flushing or cause client-visible timeouts.

Logs and traces:
- Sample trace: https://observability.internal/redwood/traces/trace-2025-01-17-22abcd
- Example serving log excerpt: 
  [2025-01-17T16:42:12Z] INFO finalizer: start finalize request SUP-531907-ctx-123
  [2025-01-17T16:42:30Z] WARN kv-cache: beginning bulk-serialize (size=512MB, entries=19278)
  [2025-01-17T16:42:55Z] ERROR api-gateway: upstream timeout after 30s while waiting for response

Additional notes:

- Node has nvme-backed local disk; serialization writes are synchronous to disk in current runtime build.
